petitioner_background: The Petitioner is a drug addiction counselor seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA section 203(b)(1)(A)). The decision does not provide details about nationality or specific career qualifications. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the underlying petition, concluding the Petitioner did not satisfy at least three of the ten initial evidentiary criteria. The Petitioner then filed a combined motion to reopen and reconsider, which the Director dismissed, leading to this appeal.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter. The AAO found that in dismissing the Petitioner's motion to reopen and reconsider, the Director provided only a summary conclusion stating the evidence did not establish the prior decision was incorrect, without providing any details to explain this conclusion. As a result, the Petitioner was unable to address any specific deficiencies on appeal. Additionally, under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(ii), the official having jurisdiction over a motion is the official who made the latest decision, so the AAO did not have jurisdiction over the motion itself. The Director must issue a new decision specifically identifying any deficiencies and explaining why the motion does not meet the requirements of a motion to reopen, reconsider, or both.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The Director dismissed the Petitioner's motion to reopen and reconsider with only a summary conclusion, failing to provide any details or specific deficiencies. This prevented the Petitioner from addressing specific issues on appeal. Additionally, the AAO determined it lacked jurisdiction over the motion itself because the official having jurisdiction over a motion is the official who made the latest decision (the Director), requiring remand.
